questions.question: Dyslexia is best described as which of the following?
options: A temporary problem that disappears in adulthood
An illness that can be cured with the right treatment
A specific learning difficulty that mainly affects reading, writing and spelling
A sign that a person is lazy or not making an effort

questions.question: Dyslexia is neurological in nature, meaning it relates to:
options: A person's eyesight being permanently damaged
The way the brain processes certain kinds of information, such as the sounds within words
A person's general level of intelligence
A short-term illness affecting concentration

questions.question: How does dyslexia vary between people?
options: It only ever affects children
It ranges from mild to more marked and is individual to each person
It is always severe
It affects everyone in exactly the same way

questions.question: What is the relationship between dyslexia and a person's intelligence?
options: Dyslexia guarantees that a person is highly intelligent
Dyslexia only occurs in people of low ability
Dyslexia is not linked to intelligence and occurs across the whole range of ability
Dyslexia means a person cannot reason well

questions.question: Dyspraxia, which can occur alongside dyslexia, mainly affects:
options: Skills with numbers
A person's hearing
Co-ordination and movement
A person's eyesight

questions.question: Dyscalculia, which sometimes co-occurs with dyslexia, affects:
options: Reading speed
Memory for faces
Skills with numbers
Balance

questions.question: Which of these best describes your role as a care worker in relation to dyslexia?
options: To teach people how to read
To be aware, adapt communication and make reasonable adjustments
To formally diagnose dyslexia in people you support
To decide who is allowed to have an assessment

questions.question: A formal identification of dyslexia is carried out by:
options: The person themselves
A person's family members
Any care worker who notices the signs
Qualified professionals such as specialist teachers or educational psychologists

questions.question: Before adapting how you share information with someone who finds reading difficult, you should:
options: Wait for a formal diagnosis of dyslexia
Ask permission from head office first
Adapt straight away whenever it helps, without waiting for a diagnosis
Do nothing until an assessment is complete

questions.question: Which of these is a common sign of dyslexia in reading?
options: Reading very quickly and always accurately
Always preferring to read aloud in groups
Never making any reading errors
Reading slowly and with effort, or losing one's place

questions.question: A common spelling sign of dyslexia is:
options: Spelling the same word several different ways
Refusing to write anything at all
Spelling every word perfectly every time
Only ever writing in capital letters

questions.question: Besides reading and writing, dyslexia can also affect a person's:
options: Appetite
Hearing
Memory, organisation and sense of time
Height

questions.question: Signs such as forgetting a sequence of spoken instructions or mixing up appointments are easily misread as:
options: Something with no link to the person at all
Excellent organisation
Carelessness or not paying attention
A sign of high intelligence

questions.question: Visual stress, sometimes called Meares-Irlen syndrome, can cause:
options: Words to appear to blur, move or run together
Loss of hearing
Permanent blindness
A raised temperature

questions.question: Which statement about visual stress is accurate?
options: It is always caused by dyslexia
It is exactly the same thing as dyslexia
It is a separate condition that can occur with or without dyslexia
It is only ever found in children

questions.question: Which of these can make visual stress worse?
options: A clear, plain font
Larger line spacing
Bright white backgrounds or screen glare
Cream or pastel coloured paper

questions.question: Years of unrecognised difficulty with reading and writing can:
options: Have no emotional effect at all
Cure the dyslexia
Knock a person's confidence and lead to anxiety or embarrassment
Improve a person's confidence over time

questions.question: People with dyslexia often show strengths such as:
options: Difficulty with absolutely every task
No practical skills whatsoever
Creativity, problem-solving and big-picture thinking
An inability to speak with others

questions.question: A strengths-based approach means:
options: Ignoring a person's difficulties completely
Focusing only on what a person finds hard
Testing the person to expose their weaknesses
Shaping tasks so a person can use what they are good at, while offering support where needed

questions.question: Under the Equality Act 2010, a disability is a physical or mental impairment that has:
options: A substantial and long-term adverse effect on normal day-to-day activities
An effect only on reading
A minor, short-term effect
No effect on daily life

questions.question: A reasonable adjustment under the Equality Act 2010 is:
options: Something only schools are required to provide
Only possible once a diagnosis has been made
A special favour with no legal basis
A sensible change that removes or reduces a barrier so a disabled person is not disadvantaged

questions.question: The duty to make reasonable adjustments falls on:
options: Employers and service providers
Only the disabled person themselves
Only a person's family
Only doctors

questions.question: Easy-read materials use:
options: Long, complex sentences and no pictures
Simple words, short sentences and supporting images
Dense blocks of unbroken text
Only technical jargon

questions.question: Which presentation choice helps a person with dyslexia or visual stress?
options: A cream or off-white background instead of bright white
Cramped text with no spacing
Long passages written in block capitals
Italics used throughout a whole document

questions.question: A coloured overlay is:
options: A tinted transparent sheet placed over a page to reduce visual stress
A type of spellchecker
A reading test given to children
A formal diagnostic test

questions.question: Dyslexia is a lifelong difference in how a person processes written language, not an illness to be cured.
options: True
False

questions.question: Difficulty with spelling is a reliable sign that a person has a low level of intelligence.
options: True
False

questions.question: Assistive technology such as text-to-speech and speech-to-text can help people with dyslexia.
options: True
False

questions.question: You must be a technology expert before you can encourage someone to use assistive technology.
options: True
False

questions.question: Breaking a large task into smaller, single steps can make it more manageable for someone with dyslexia.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is good practice to ask a person with dyslexia to read aloud in front of others to build their confidence.
options: True
False

questions.question: Information about a person's dyslexia should be treated as confidential and not shared without good reason and the person's agreement.
options: True
False

questions.question: If a person finds one thing hard, you can safely assume they will struggle with everything.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are common signs associated with dyslexia? (Select all that apply)
options: Reading slowly and losing one's place
Spelling the same word several different ways
Difficulty remembering a sequence of spoken instructions
Always spelling every word correctly
Never having any difficulty with organisation

questions.question: Which of the following are helpful reasonable adjustments for a person with dyslexia? (Select all that apply)
options: Allowing extra time for reading or forms
Insisting the person reads quickly and unaided
Refusing help until a diagnosis has been shown
Providing information in an easy-read format
Offering instructions verbally as well as in writing

questions.question: Dyslexia commonly co-occurs with which of the following? (Select all that apply)
options: Dyspraxia
Attention difficulties such as ADHD
A broken bone
Dyscalculia

questions.question: Which of these are strengths often seen in people with dyslexia? (Select all that apply)
options: Creativity and original problem-solving
Strong verbal, social or practical skills
An inability to work with other people
Seeing the bigger picture

questions.question: Which of the following help make written information easier to read? (Select all that apply)
options: An off-white or pastel background
A clear, plain font at a comfortable size
Good line spacing and clear headings
Long passages written in block capitals
Dense, cramped paragraphs

questions.question: Which of these are examples of assistive technology that can support people with dyslexia? (Select all that apply)
options: Text-to-speech software
A formal diagnosis certificate
Speech-to-text dictation
Reminder apps

questions.question: Which of the following help protect the dignity of a person with dyslexia? (Select all that apply)
options: Never forcing someone to read aloud in front of others
Giving people warning and time to prepare
Helping with forms discreetly, away from an audience
Springing tasks on people without any warning

questions.question: Dyslexia is a specific learning difficulty and is not linked to a person's ___.
The Equality Act 2010 places a duty on employers and service providers to make reasonable ___ so that disabled people are not put at a disadvantage.
Some people with dyslexia experience visual ___, where words may appear to blur or move on the page.
A person-centred approach means asking the individual what helps them, because the person themselves is the ___ in what works for them.
A well-known source of information, guidance and helplines is the British ___ Association.
